title: The FY27 Financial Architecture Most Owner-Led Businesses Build Too Late
linkedin:
Most Australian owner-led businesses start FY27 tomorrow the same way they ended FY26: reacting.

The accountant needs the FY26 documents. The tax return is months away. July absorbs the same operational demands as June did, and the decisions that shape the whole year ahead get made from intuition rather than structure.

By October, the business is three months in without a clear profit target, a cash forecast, or a monthly reporting rhythm that would show whether things are tracking or quietly drifting. At that point FY27 is already a quarter gone.

What changes when there is a financial partner in the room from July 1:

The FY26 accounts get turned into a working FY27 budget within two weeks, not in September when the compliance picture lands. The 13-week cash view is updated with the new year's opening position so the July superannuation payment and the August revenue build are visible before they arrive. The monthly reporting cadence is set before anyone has made commitments the numbers haven't confirmed.

The structure gets built in the window where it does the most good: before the year's decisions have already been made.

This is not a complex setup. It is a series of specific activities that happen in July when someone is responsible for doing them, and get deferred or skipped when no one is.

We've written about this on the ProfitPulse blog this week, covering what the first two weeks of July look like when there is a commercial financial framework in place from day one of the new year, and why starting on July 1 rather than October 1 compounds through the whole of FY27.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog. Link in the first comment.

#FractionalCFO #SMEFinance #AustralianBusiness
facebook:
As FY27 opens tomorrow, a quick question for owner-led business owners:

Does your business have an annual budget, a cash forecast, and a monthly reporting rhythm ready to go from July 1?

For most businesses the honest answer is no. The financial structure gets built later in the year, or it never quite gets built at all, and FY27 runs on the same rhythms as FY26.

The start of a new financial year is genuinely the cleanest window to change that. When it happens in October instead, three months of decisions have already been made without a plan to measure them against.

New post on the ProfitPulse blog about what actually gets set up in July when there is a financial partner in the room. Link goes up when the post is live.

#AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
google:
With FY27 opening on 1 July, ProfitPulse is working with owner-led businesses across Queensland and NSW on the financial setup that shapes how the whole year performs.

Most businesses start the new financial year without an annual budget for FY27, a cash flow forecast covering the first quarter, or a monthly management accounts rhythm that would show how the business is tracking against a plan. These structures get built later in the year, or they do not get built at all.

When they are built in July, using the complete FY26 accounts now available, they work from the cleanest possible data and get established before the year's decisions have already been made. A budget built in October reflects choices already locked in. A budget built in July shapes the choices still ahead.

For Brisbane, South East Queensland, and East Coast businesses thinking about what financial structure they actually need heading into FY27, the new post on the ProfitPulse blog covers what gets built in the first two weeks of July when a financial partner is in the room, and why that window matters more than most owners realise.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
